Multiple reports say U.S. allies believe the Strait of Hormuz can’t be reopened by force or threats.

Israeli officials, however, expect the Trump administration to apply heavy pressure or act forcefull to reopen it. This may not happen, but it’s what Israel is preparing for.

Parental satisfaction with their child's actual school reached its low point in 2013, which was also the year that NAEP scores peaked. 

Things have gotten worse since then, and people seem somewhat happier with the worse results.

Very smart take on what went wrong in the past, and exactly the kind of pledge to be different from a generic baseline Democrat that you need to win in tough races.

Video

Solar progress depends on sunshine, transmission infrastructure, and the ability to obtain large parcels of land — it's not about anti-fossil fuel regulations.


Daniel Gross (@grossdm)

Pretty astonishing. In Texas, between 10:00 am and 4:00 p.m., 80-90% of electricity comes from carbon free sources. And storage is already a significant contributor in the early morning and evening





— https://nitter.net/grossdm/status/2034608922753982862#m

Liel Leibovitz in the Free Press:  "As a work of art, One Battle After Another is irredeemable. It feels like the sort of thing written by a committee of socialist college sophomores cracking each other up ...  

"Paul Thomas Anderson seems interested more in purring for his fellow progressives than in making interesting movies ..."

Trump approval is now below where he was in his first term. 

But views of Dems are worse than they were in 2018; definite need to try to come up with ways to do a "new look." 

natesilver.net/p/trump-appro…

I feel like nobody knows this but American math performance at the very top end has actually gotten better even while everyone else has gotten worse. 

the74million.org/article/how…

The choice here ultimately is exactly the one we started with. 

Is it more important to get to yes on clean energy, or to maximize opportunities to say no to fossil fuels. 

Bapna wants to keep prioritizing "no" while making it seem like he is disavowing that priority.

The whole purpose of this article, ultimately, is to spike bipartisan permitting reform efforts by painting the permitting reform talks as solely a weapon of the oil industry. 

But ask the American Council on Renewable Energy — they say we need permitting reform!
Again, forget any controversies about fossil fuels — these ideas about how to make it harder and more expensive to do utility-scale renewables.
Even within the specific context of renewable energy projects, there is a concern that it may become *too easy* to build large-scale solar and wind and aligned transmission.

Perfect example — here in the first graf he appears to be acknowledging that the environmental review process can be counterproductive. 

But when he talks about the reforms that the fossil fuel industry is blocking, none of them are reforms to environmental review!

But if you read the text of the article, there is almost no *actual* change here. 

What's happening here is brand management, he wants Dems who agree with the high-level critique to still care about his group so he's pretending to agree with it.

I think JON OSSOFF might have the best chance of being a candidate who gets real credit from progressives for breaking with Biden on arms sales to Israel without having a particularly left-wing record. 

The opposite of the Newsom/Harris profile. 

www.slowboring.com/p/the-obama-...
My intuition aligns with yours, but the research indicates that we are wrong which is why I thought it was interesting to write about.
No I'm not, ask the authors if you want.
Say the Supreme Court got it right and the government should enforce that.

Notable to the discourse, moderating on trans issues is not particularly high-leverage. 

The best things to move right on are (in order) racial preferences, teachers unions, sexual orientation curriculum in schools, letting in asylum-seekers, punishing non-violent criminals, and fossil fuels.

So the upshot here is that rather than moderating at random, Democrats should stick with conventional progressive defense of Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid while also adopting views that are to the right of the entire existing caucus on a few key issues.

What is the single most effective issue to move to the center on? 

Affirmative action in college admissions — an issue that no Democrat in recent cycles has been moderate on. 

Obviously moderation doesn't work if you don't do it!

And to be clear "moderate" in this research does not mean "like Kamala Harris or Gavin Newsom as opposed to Bernie" — it means "be more right-wing than mainstream establishment Democrats." 

But it does also help explain why the observed benefits of moderation in recent cycles are small.

Sorry to give a less measured take, this research indicates that just adopting more moderate views at random — though definitely not optimal — will on average help you win elections. 

If you have no survey research or data or anything, just go with your gut and be more moderate.

As Morris has documented extensively, the electoral benefits of moderation, though real, are also not enormous. 

The paper suggests that moderating SELECTIVELY — on affirmative action, merit pay for teachers, punishing non-violent crime, and using fossil fuels — would be much more potent.

As @gelliottmorris.com says, the research offers more nuanced advice than "just be more moderate." 

It says that moderation is beneficial on average, but that the impact of moderation varies issue-to-issue and that on a minority of issues moderation actually backfires.
I promise that I discussed this paper with the authors, that's why I had exclusive early access to write a New York Times op-ed about it.
I like that he's for school prayer AND for sex stores

The YIMBY movement has come to encompass a wide range of themes and ideas — single stair, missing middle, manufactured homes — and achieved national scope but the core question of growth (or lack thereof) in a handful of very high cost areas remains crucial. 

www.slowboring.com/p/why-silico...
In my defense, the text was wrapped up days ago
If I had a barrel of West Texas Intermediate crude oil laying around somewhere, I would not sell it for $100 given the current circumstances.
I think Caraballo is just restating the problem here not offering an alternative solution.

Today in the NYT, I discuss some fresh research on which moderations move the most voters.

www.nytimes.com/2026/03/16/o...
This is an amazing platform

If per person spending on the elderly goes up and the elderly share of the population also goes up, everything else gets squeezed and everyone is mad.

But it’s what the voters say they want.
